metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend `tests/unit/test_deprecation_registry.py` (or add a parallel tombstone-completeness test) to source-scan for inline `RemovedInputError` raises that bypass the new `RemovedInput` registry, matching the pattern already used for the deprecation completeness check.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the existing `test_deprecation_registry.py` source-scanner enforces that 'a new deprecated surface that skips the registry is caught' — but the scanner only checks for raw `logger.warning(…deprecated…)` / `warnings.warn(…, DeprecationWarning)` emissions. A new `RemovedInputError` raise path (the tombstone signal) does NOT use `warn_deprecated` and is NOT a deprecation-warning emission, so the existing completeness scanner would never catch a tombstone site that bypasses the new `RemovedInput` registry. The plan adds no mention of extending the source-scan test to cover tombstone emissions.
findings.location: Scope / tests/unit/ section; What bullet 'Distinct, un-swallowable error type'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact insertion point in `src/rebar/llm/config.py` (e.g., at the top of `LLMConfig.from_env()` before env-var reads, analogous to the `load_config()` placement for core vars) for the `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` tombstone, since no active code currently handles this variable and the Scope citation 'LLM config load path' is ambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan classifies `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` as a tombstone to be 'applied in the LLM config load path (see Scope)' and states it 'lives in the optional `rebar.llm` layer (not `config.py`/`_config_schema.py`)'. A search of `src/rebar/llm/config.py` finds zero occurrences of `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` — the variable no longer exists in any active code path and appears only in a historical comment in `_deprecations.py`. There is no identifiable 'LLM config load path' that reads `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` today, meaning the tombstone placement in the Scope section references a code location that does not currently handle this variable.
findings.location: What section — 'REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS→REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS' bullet; Scope bullet 'src/rebar/llm/ (LLM config load path)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add the tombstone check at the top of `LLMConfig.from_env()` in `src/rebar/llm/config.py`, before any `os.environ.get("REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS", …)` read, mirroring exactly the `load_config()` placement prescribed for core-config tombstones.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that TOML error-class tombstones in `_config_schema.py` must also raise `RemovedInputError` (not `ConfigError`) so `tracker_dir()`'s `except ConfigError` guard does not swallow them — either by importing and raising `RemovedInputError` from `_config_schema.py`, or by routing TOML tombstone detection through the same pre-cache check in `load_config()` that catches env tombstones.
findings.finding: TOML error-class tombstones are routed through _config_schema.py which only raises ConfigError — but tracker_dir()'s 'except ConfigError' guard (config.py:119-124) will silently swallow any ConfigError, re-creating the exact silent-redirect the plan exists to prevent for the TOML tombstone case (e.g., verify.require_verdict_for_close in rebar.toml).
findings.location: What / 'Distinct, un-swallowable error type' bullet; Scope / '_config_schema.py' line; AC line 'The error-class tombstone raises a RemovedInputError that is not a ConfigError subclass'
findings.suggested_fix: Move TOML tombstone detection out of coerce_sparse() and into the same pre-cache block at the top of load_config() (after parsing the raw TOML layers but before returning the Config). This keeps all error-class tombstone raises in one place, all raising RemovedInputError, and all outside the tracker_dir() ConfigError guard.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` tombstone integrates with the main `RemovedInput` registry: either (a) it is registered in `_deprecations.py` and the LLM load path calls the shared dispatcher, or (b) the LLM path has its own parallel tombstone table — and state which type (`RemovedInputError` or other) is raised and whether `rebar config validate` covers it.
findings.finding: The `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` tombstone is placed in `src/rebar/llm/` (the LLM config load path), which uses completely separate resolution machinery (`_llm_int`/`_llm_str`/`_read_llm_file_table`, not `load_config()`). The plan does not specify whether this tombstone uses the same `RemovedInput` registry and `RemovedInputError` type, or a parallel mechanism — and since the LLM path never calls `load_config()`, the pre-cache-check placement in `load_config()` that the plan mandates for other tombstones does not apply here. No integration protocol between the LLM-path tombstone and the main registry is specified.
findings.location: What section — 'REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS→REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS lives in the optional rebar.llm layer … so its tombstone is applied in the LLM config load path'; Scope — 'src/rebar/llm/ (LLM config load path) — apply the REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS tombstone there'

findings.finding: `tracker_dir()` in `config.py` wraps `load_config()` in a `try/except ConfigError` that silently falls back to the default `.tickets-tracker` path on any `ConfigError`. If the tombstone raises a `ConfigError`-derived exception from inside `load_config()`, it will be swallowed here — exactly the silent-redirect failure mode the plan is designed to prevent for `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR`. The plan does not address this swallowing path.
findings.location: `src/rebar/config.py` `tracker_dir()` function / plan 'What' section (cache-safe placement)
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a distinct `TombstoneError(SystemExit)` or re-raise via `sys.exit()` rather than `ConfigError`, so it is not caught by `tracker_dir()`'s broad `except ConfigError` guard.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify `RemovedInputError`'s base class: state explicitly whether it should subclass `SystemExit` (BaseException family), a plain `Exception` subclass handled at the CLI entry point, or a `BaseException` subclass — and specify where the top-level handler converts it to a nonzero exit.
findings.finding: `RemovedInputError` is described as 'a `SystemExit`/standalone' type, leaving the executor to decide between literally subclassing `SystemExit` (which Python catches differently — e.g. a bare `except Exception` does NOT catch it) versus a plain standalone exception class not inheriting from `ConfigError`. This is an architectural decision with runtime propagation implications that the plan does not settle.
findings.location: What: 'A retired error-class input raises a dedicated `RemovedInputError` that is **NOT** a subclass of `ConfigError` (make it a `SystemExit`/standalone).'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `class RemovedInputError(RuntimeError)` (a plain Exception subclass, not ConfigError), and add an explicit `except RemovedInputError` handler at the CLI entry point that calls `sys.exit(1)` with the formatted message. This is the least-surprising pattern and satisfies the 'not swallowed by except ConfigError' requirement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and test behavior when both the tombstoned input and its replacement are set simultaneously (e.g., TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR + REBAR_TRACKER_DIR both present) — options include: suppress tombstone error if replacement is also set (with a downgraded warning), or keep the error and note the replacement is already set.
findings.finding: When both a tombstoned (old) input AND its replacement (new) input are set simultaneously — a realistic in-flight migration state — the plan does not specify the behavior: does the tombstone still fire (potentially confusing an operator who already set the new var), or is the old var silently ignored when the new one is present?
findings.location: What section — bullet list of seeded inputs; Acceptance Criteria bullet 2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and test the `rebar config validate` exit code for warn-only tombstoned inputs (e.g., exit 0 with warning lines on stderr, or a distinct exit code such as 2 for 'warnings present') and add that scenario to AC bullet 6's test assertions.
findings.finding: The `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` command's exit-code behavior when only warn-level tombstoned inputs are present (e.g., REBAR_PUSH set but no error-class input) is not specified. AC bullet 6 only covers the error-class case (TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR → non-zero) and the clean case (→ 0); the warn-only scenario is a gap.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullet 6; What section — 'Add a rebar config validate / rebar doctor command'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test (and specify the intended behavior in the plan) for the case where a tombstoned env var AND its replacement are both set simultaneously — for each error-class entry, assert whether the tombstone fires or is suppressed, and verify the store/network invariants still hold.
findings.finding: The testing plan never specifies behavior or a test for the scenario where BOTH a tombstoned input (e.g. TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR) AND its replacement (REBAR_TRACKER_DIR) are set simultaneously — a real migration scenario for a security-sensitive surface. The plan's AC2 explicitly uses 'sets only that input', so this combination is never exercised. The tombstone could fire (breaking a correctly-migrated deployment that forgot to unset the old var) or be suppressed (possibly masking an operator error), with no AC specifying which is correct.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2; What section (env, error entries for TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR, REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE)
findings.suggested_fix: Define the intended semantics (recommendation: tombstone fires only when the OLD name is set AND the NEW name is absent; if the new name is already set, emit a warn-level 'old name is redundant, please remove it' rather than a hard error). Add one sub-row per error-class tombstone entry to the table-driven test asserting this semantics.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-display contract for `RemovedInputError`: either (a) it is caught at the CLI top-level dispatcher and formatted to stderr before `sys.exit(1)`, naming the required fields (old name, replacement, removed-in, migration example), or (b) it prints to stderr in its own `__init__` / a helper and then raises `SystemExit(1)` — and document which pattern the AC tests should verify.
findings.finding: The plan specifies RemovedInputError as 'a SystemExit/standalone' (the slash implies either) but does not specify the mechanism by which the formatted error message (old name + replacement + removed-in + migration example) reaches the user's stderr before process exit — leaving the implementer to choose between incompatible designs that satisfy the spec's letter but not its intent.
findings.location: What / 'Distinct, un-swallowable error type' bullet; AC line 'fails with a nonzero exit and a message naming the old name, the replacement, and the removed-in release'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `RemovedInputError` base class: specify whether it is a plain `Exception` subclass (converted to `sys.exit(1)` at CLI/MCP entry points, matching codebase convention) or a `SystemExit` subclass (explain why and how it interacts with the MCP server's `except Exception` top-level handler).
findings.finding: The plan specifies `RemovedInputError` as 'a `SystemExit`/standalone' type (the `/` expressing unresolved ambiguity between two architecturally different choices) but does not verify which is correct. `class RemovedInputError(SystemExit)` and `class RemovedInputError(Exception)` + `sys.exit(1)` at the CLI boundary behave differently: `SystemExit` subclasses escape `except Exception` entirely (including top-level loggers/traceback formatters), while a standalone exception can be caught, logged, and cleanly converted at the boundary — the codebase's established pattern (`RebarError`, `ConfigError`, `ConcurrencyError` are all `Exception` subclasses, converted to `raise SystemExit(1)` at CLI entry points in `src/rebar/_cli/_init.py`). The plan's rationale (bypassing `except ConfigError` in `tracker_dir()`) is satisfied by EITHER approach, making the `SystemExit` subclass unnecessary.
findings.location: What section — 'A retired error-class input raises a dedicated `RemovedInputError` that is NOT a subclass of `ConfigError` (make it a `SystemExit`/standalone)'
findings.suggested_fix: Define `class RemovedInputError(Exception)` (not `SystemExit`), ensure CLI entry points catch it alongside `ConfigError` and call `sys.exit(1)`, and note in the plan that `tracker_dir()`'s `except ConfigError` already does NOT catch it — satisfying the bypass requirement without deviating from the codebase's exception architecture.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `rebar config validate` handles a `RemovedInputError` raised during its own config-load phase — e.g. 'validate catches `RemovedInputError` and formats its `.name`/`.replacement`/`.removed_in` fields into the structured report, then exits non-zero' — so AC6 has a concrete implementation path.
findings.finding: The `rebar config validate` command is specified to 'inspect process env, project TOML, user TOML, and file-supplied config against the active aliases and tombstones', but the plan does not specify a fallback for the bootstrapping conflict: when a hard-error tombstone fires at the top of `load_config()` (before the cache lookup), `validate` cannot load config to report it — `load_config()` raises `RemovedInputError` before `validate` can inspect the TOML layers. No handling of this case is described.
findings.location: What section — 'Add a rebar config validate / rebar doctor command that inspects process env, project TOML, user TOML'; AC6 — 'TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR=old rebar config validate exits non-zero and prints the old name + replacement + removed-in'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope or AC6: 'The `validate` command wraps its inspection in a `try/except RemovedInputError as e` block, formats `e.name + e.replacement + e.removed_in` into the report, and exits non-zero — it does NOT re-raise, so the structured output is always produced even when a hard-error tombstone is present.'

findings.finding: AC5 specifies that `rebar config validate` exits non-zero when an error-class tombstone is present (example: `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR`) and exits 0 in a clean environment, but says nothing about the exit code when only warn-class tombstones are found — leaving an ambiguous contract for the new command.
findings.location: AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC5: 'Setting a warn-class retired input (e.g., `REBAR_PUSH=old rebar config validate`) exits 0 and prints a warning naming the old name, replacement, and removed-in release.'

findings.finding: The `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` command's exit-code behavior when it finds only warn-level (inert) tombstones is not specified. The AC covers exit non-zero for an error-level tombstone (`TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR`) and exit 0 for a clean environment, but the warn-level case is absent. Operators scripting around this command (e.g., in CI pre-flight) need a deterministic answer.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC for `rebar config validate`; 'What' section — warn-only for operationally inert retired inputs
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'Setting only an operationally-inert retired input (e.g., `REBAR_PUSH=1 rebar config validate`) exits 0 with a warning printed to stderr naming the old name and replacement — not non-zero — so scripted health checks are not broken by warn-level findings.'

findings.finding: The `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` command has no specified behavior for non-happy-path inputs: malformed TOML, unreadable config files, missing project vs user TOML, and the command's own exit-code semantics (0 vs nonzero when tombstones-only-warnings found vs errors found vs clean) are all absent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC bullet 5 / Scope — 'New CLI subcommand'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC bullet 5: 'Exit codes: 0 = no issues; 1 = deprecated-but-warn inputs found; 2 = tombstoned (error-behavior) inputs found or config file unreadable/malformed. Malformed TOML and unreadable files emit a parse-error diagnostic and exit 2. Missing optional config files (user TOML, project TOML) are treated as empty and noted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan (or in a Scope note) that warn-class tombstone signals must route through a new `signal_tombstone()` helper (or equivalent) that the `test_no_raw_deprecation_emissions` source-scanner recognizes as an approved emission path — and add that helper/module to the Scope.
findings.finding: The existing `test_deprecation_registry.py` source-scanner (`test_no_raw_deprecation_emissions`) walks all `.py` files under `src/rebar/` and flags any `logger.warning(...)` or `warnings.warn(..., DeprecationWarning)` call whose string literal contains 'deprecat'. If the tombstone check implementation emits a `logger.warning` message containing 'deprecated' (e.g. for warn-class tombstones) without routing through `warn_deprecated`, it will trip this scanner and break the build. The plan does not call out this constraint.
findings.location: What / 'Warn-only for operationally inert retired inputs'; tests/unit/test_deprecation_registry.py:125-133

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequence: e.g., a `REBAR_IGNORE_TOMBSTONES` escape-hatch env var (or a warn-first release followed by a hard-error release), and document the operator recovery path for each error-class tombstone.
findings.finding: The plan converts previously-silent or warn-only behavior into hard nonzero exits for operators with stale deployment manifests. This is an explicitly justified breaking change, but no rollback path, back-out mechanism, or expand-contract sequence is described — the plan only justifies why the break is correct, not how to recover if an operator's deployment hard-fails unexpectedly.
findings.location: What section and Acceptance Criteria overall

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether tombstone checks are collect-all (report every violation before exit) or fail-fast (abort on first), and add a test asserting the behavior when two or more error-class tombstoned inputs are set simultaneously.
findings.finding: When multiple error-class tombstoned inputs are set at once, the plan and AC only test one retired input at a time ('sets only that input'), leaving it unspecified whether the process reports all violations before exiting (collect-all) or aborts on the first (fail-fast). An operator with several stale env vars must fix them one at a time with no visibility into the remaining issues.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullet 2; What section — cache-safe placement description
findings.suggested_fix: Prefer collect-all: run all env-var and TOML tombstone checks, accumulate errors, then raise a single combined RemovedInputError listing all violations. This is the pattern used by linters and config validators (e.g. OpenSSH counts bad options before terminating).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `'file'` to `_KINDS` in `src/rebar/_deprecations.py` (or document that the tombstone registry uses a separate kind-set) so the existing `test_registry_entries_well_formed` well-formedness assertion does not break when the `file`-kind `.rebar/config.conf` tombstone is added.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a `file` kind for tombstones (the `.rebar/config.conf` presence check) added to `_deprecations.py`, but the existing `_KINDS = frozenset({'env','cfg','cli','lib','mcp'})` in that module does not include `'file'`. The existing `test_registry_entries_well_formed` test asserts `entry.kind in dep._KINDS` — adding a `file`-kind tombstone entry without also extending `_KINDS` will break that test.
findings.location: What / Seed registry / 'file, error' tombstone; src/rebar/_deprecations.py:42; tests/unit/test_deprecation_registry.py:37
findings.suggested_fix: Extend _KINDS to frozenset({'env','cfg','cli','lib','mcp','file'}) and note this in the Scope under _deprecations.py.

findings.finding: The plan proposes `rebar config validate` as the new command, but `rebar config` is already an intercepted special case in `_cli/__init__.py` that routes unconditionally to `show_config.config_cli(argv[1:])`. The `show_config.py` file is not in the plan's Scope, yet adding a `validate` sub-subcommand there is required to make `rebar config validate` work. Alternatively, a top-level `rebar doctor` command would require a new help file in `_cli/help/` and updated golden files in `tests/golden/cli_help/` — neither of which appears in the Scope.
findings.location: Scope section / `New CLI subcommand (config validate / doctor) + wiring` / AC line 5
findings.suggested_fix: Expand the Scope to name: `src/rebar/_commands/show_config.py` (add `validate` subcommand parsing), a new `src/rebar/_cli/help/config.txt` or `doctor.txt`, updated `src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt`, and the corresponding `tests/golden/cli_help/` golden files.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS to the warn-class sub-table in AC bullet 3 (or a dedicated AC bullet) verifying that setting it in the LLM config load path emits a warning and exits 0.
findings.finding: REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS tombstone is a named deliverable in Scope ('src/rebar/llm/ — apply the REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS tombstone there') and What ('classified warn') but no AC bullet tests it — neither the error-class table-driven test (AC bullet 2) nor the warn sub-table (AC bullet 3) lists it as a covered case.
findings.location: Scope section ('src/rebar/llm/') and AC bullets 2–3
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC bullet 3's sub-table: 'REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS→REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS (LLM config load path): exit 0, stderr contains old name + replacement.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a developer-facing note (ADR or CLAUDE.md entry) capturing the architectural decision that `RemovedInputError` is intentionally NOT a `ConfigError` subclass, explaining why (to prevent swallowing in `except ConfigError` guards), so future exception-handler authors know to preserve this contract.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `RemovedInputError` as a type that deliberately sits outside the `ConfigError` hierarchy — a cross-cutting exception contract that every future exception handler in config.py, _config_schema.py, llm/, CLI, and any new caller must respect — but no ADR or CLAUDE.md update is called out to capture this design decision for maintainers.
findings.location: What section / 'Distinct, un-swallowable error type' paragraph; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (e.g. `docs/adr/0NNN-removed-input-error-hierarchy.md`) or a CLAUDE.md section under 'Exception hierarchy conventions' stating: '`RemovedInputError` is a direct `SystemExit`/standalone exception, NOT a `ConfigError` subclass. This is intentional: `tracker_dir()` and similar callers catch `ConfigError` and fall back silently; a tombstone error must propagate past those guards. Any future handler for config errors must NOT catch `RemovedInputError` unless it re-raises or exits non-zero.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit all call-sites of `load_config()` (beyond `tracker_dir()`) for broad exception handlers that would swallow `RemovedInputError`, and either name them as safe (no catch-all) or add them to the Scope with required changes.
findings.finding: The plan changes the exception contract of `load_config()` (new `RemovedInputError` can now propagate from it), but only acknowledges one caller — `tracker_dir()` with its `except ConfigError` — as a dependent of the old behavior. Other callers of `load_config()` that may have their own broad `except` guards or that pass its result directly to user-facing surfaces are not surveyed.
findings.location: What section — 'Distinct, un-swallowable error type'; Scope — config.py; Acceptance Criteria bullet 4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` and `docs/env-vars.md` to the plan's Scope, and specify how the env-var tombstone entries will be surfaced in the generated env-var registry (either by extending `_env_aliases()` to read the new tombstone registry, or by ensuring tombstone entries land in `_deprecations.REGISTRY` where the generator already finds them).
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` derives `docs/env-vars.md` from `_deprecations.REGISTRY` (filtering `dep.kind == 'env'`). If the new tombstone entries live in a separate registry structure rather than as `Deprecation` entries in `REGISTRY`, env-var tombstones (`TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE`, etc.) will be absent from `docs/env-vars.md` and the CI drift gate (`test_check_mode_clean_against_committed_tree`) will fail because the committed `docs/env-vars.md` won't reflect the new tombstoned variables. The plan does not enumerate `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` or `docs/env-vars.md` as edit targets.
findings.location: Scope section; scripts/gen_env_registry.py:127-140; tests/unit/test_gen_env_registry.py:59

findings.finding: The plan describes `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS`→`REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS` as a seeded tombstone entry applied in the LLM config load path (not `config.py`), but AC2's verify step invokes 'config load / the smallest CLI command that initialises config' — which likely does not exercise the LLM config load path. This leaves `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` as described work with no effectively-verifiable acceptance criterion.
findings.location: What section (REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS bullet) and AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2 with a sentence: 'For `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS`, invoke the LLM config load entry-point (e.g., `rebar.llm.load_llm_config()`) with the env var set and assert warn-level message containing old name + replacement.'

findings.finding: AC5 for the new `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` command only specifies testing one env-var tombstone path (`TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR=old`) and a clean-environment path; it does not specify test cases for the TOML tombstone path (e.g., a project TOML containing `verify.require_verdict_for_close`) or the file-presence tombstone path (a leftover `.rebar/config.conf`), both of which are advertised behaviors of the validate command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 (`rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` command)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC5: 'Also verified by: (a) a test that writes a project TOML with `verify.require_verdict_for_close = true` and runs `rebar config validate`, asserting nonzero exit + old-key + replacement in output; (b) a test that creates a `.rebar/config.conf` file and runs `rebar config validate`, asserting nonzero exit + migration message.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the command name fork: choose one name (`rebar config validate` OR `rebar doctor`) and use it consistently in What, Scope, AC6, and the proving command — or explicitly add both as aliases with the primary stated.
findings.finding: The plan defers the command name decision to the executor: 'rebar config validate' and 'rebar doctor' appear interchangeably throughout What, Scope, and AC6 with no stated default or rationale for choosing one. An agent cannot write the CLI wiring, tests, or proving command without resolving this fork.
findings.location: What (add a `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` command); Scope ('New CLI subcommand (`config validate` / `doctor`)'); AC6 proving command (`TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR=old rebar config validate`)
findings.suggested_fix: Choose `rebar config validate` as the canonical subcommand (it is the more specific, naturally scoped name); update AC6 proving command to `rebar config validate` and remove `rebar doctor` or note it as a secondary alias.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specified proving mechanism for the 'no network call' sub-criterion in AC2 (e.g., patch the network layer with `unittest.mock.patch` on the relevant outbound call sites, or use `pytest-socket` to disable sockets in those tests and assert no `SocketBlockedError` is raised).
findings.finding: AC2 requires 'no network call' as a sub-criterion but specifies no proving mechanism: it does not say whether the test uses a network mock/patch, a loopback monitor, or a process-level assertion. Without a specified approach, the sub-criterion is not deterministically verifiable in-session.
findings.location: AC2: '…does not mutate the store or reach the network — verified by a table-driven test that … asserts nonzero exit + old-name + replacement in the message + no store write (store files byte-unchanged) + no network call.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'no network call — verified by patching `httpx.Client.send` / `requests.Session.send` (or the project's HTTP client) to raise `AssertionError` if called, and asserting no such call occurs during the test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC2 how 'store files byte-unchanged' is verified: name the store root path used in tests (e.g., a tmp directory set via `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` in the test fixture), and state the comparison mechanism (e.g., assert the directory does not exist / remains empty after the tombstone fires).
findings.finding: AC2 states 'store files byte-unchanged' as a sub-criterion but does not specify which directory or files constitute 'the store', nor how their pre/post state is compared. Without knowing the store root (e.g., `.tickets-tracker/`, a path from `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR`), the proving command cannot be written.
findings.location: AC2: '…no store write (store files byte-unchanged)…'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'store byte-unchanged — verified by setting `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` to a fresh `tmp_path` fixture directory in each test case and asserting `list(tmp_path.iterdir()) == []` after the tombstone fires.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the `.rebar/config.conf` file-presence tombstone as an explicit case in AC bullet 2's table-driven test, asserting nonzero exit + message naming rebar.toml replacement when the file exists.
findings.finding: The file-presence tombstone for `.rebar/config.conf` is classified as 'file, error' in the What section, making it an error-class input, but AC bullet 2's table-driven test enumerates only env vars and TOML keys (TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR, REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE, verify.require_verdict_for_close) — the file tombstone is absent from the error-class AC coverage.
findings.location: What section ('file, error' entry) and AC bullet 2
findings.suggested_fix: In AC bullet 2, extend the 'e.g.' list to include 'presence of .rebar/config.conf (file-presence tombstone)' and assert nonzero exit + replacement message.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `reconciler.lock_max_retries` to the explicit list in AC bullet 3's warn sub-table to ensure its warn classification is verified.
findings.finding: `reconciler.lock_max_retries` is listed as a 'cfg, warn' entry in the What seed registry but is absent from the warn-class sub-table in AC bullet 3, which mentions only `REBAR_PUSH`, `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_MAX_RETRIES`, and `reconciler.lock_backend`.
findings.location: What section (seed registry, 'cfg, warn') and AC bullet 3
findings.suggested_fix: In AC bullet 3, change '(REBAR_PUSH, REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_MAX_RETRIES, reconciler.lock_backend, …)' to include 'reconciler.lock_max_retries'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Scope section where `RemovedInputError` will be defined (e.g., a new `src/rebar/_exceptions.py`, or in `_deprecations.py`) and acknowledge/mitigate the resulting import coupling (e.g., note that all config-layer modules importing from `_deprecations.py` is acceptable because it has no upward dependencies).
findings.finding: The Scope section enumerates files to modify but does not specify where the new `RemovedInputError` type will be defined, leaving the home module unresolved; if it is in `_deprecations.py` that creates an import coupling from `config.py`, `_config_schema.py`, `llm/`, CLI, and tests all into `_deprecations.py`, but if it belongs in a new exceptions module, that module is missing from Scope.
findings.location: Scope section; 'Distinct, un-swallowable error type' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/_exceptions.py` (or confirm `_deprecations.py`) to the Scope file list with a one-line rationale: '`RemovedInputError` lives here because `_deprecations.py` has no upward imports and is already the authority on retired names; `config.py`, `_config_schema.py`, and `llm/` may import from it without circularity.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to a single canonical command name (`rebar config validate` or `rebar doctor`) throughout the plan, What section, Scope, and all ACs — remove the `/ rebar doctor` (or `/ rebar config validate`) alternative to prevent stale doc references.
findings.finding: Throughout the plan and ACs, the new CLI subcommand is named both `rebar config validate` and `rebar doctor` interchangeably (using `/`), but only one command can be canonical; the plan does not commit to a name, which will leave docs/config.md and AC references stale for whichever variant isn't implemented.
findings.location: What section ('Add a `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` command'); AC 6; Scope ('New CLI subcommand (`config validate` / `doctor`)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why `RemovedInput` must be a parallel dataclass rather than extending `Deprecation` with `removed: bool` and `behavior: Literal['error','warn']` fields — or, if extending is chosen, confirm the existing `test_no_raw_deprecation_emissions` scanner is also extended to cover `RemovedInputError` raise sites.
findings.finding: The proposed `RemovedInput` tombstone dataclass duplicates almost every field of the existing `Deprecation` dataclass in `_deprecations.py` (`kind`, `name`, `replacement`, `removed_in` ≅ `remove_in`, and implicitly `key`), with only `behavior = error|warn` as a net-new field. The plan does not justify why this must be a parallel, separately-typed registry rather than extending the existing `Deprecation` type (e.g. adding `removed: bool = False` and `behavior: str = 'warn'`) and reusing the existing `REGISTRY` dict and `warn_deprecated` infrastructure — which already has the `kind`, key-lookup, and emission-channel machinery.
findings.location: What section / `_deprecations.py` Scope bullet — 'add the tombstone registry alongside the alias registry'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `Deprecation` with two new fields (`removed: bool = False`, `behavior: str = 'warn'`) and a factory `_removed(kind, name, replacement, *, removed_in, behavior='error')`. Reuse the existing `REGISTRY` dict; route tombstone-check lookup through it. Add a parallel source-scan test that walks for `RemovedInputError` raises outside the designated emission site to match the existing `test_no_raw_deprecation_emissions` pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `rebar config validate` vs. `rebar doctor` ambiguity — confirm whether tombstone reporting is a subcommand of the existing `rebar config` (preferred, avoids new top-level surface) or a new top-level `doctor` command, and remove the other name from the plan and AC.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new CLI command surface (`rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor`) without deciding between the two names and without explaining why the tombstone-reporting behavior cannot be folded into the already-existing `rebar config` command (`show_config.py` / `_commands/show_config.py`). The existing command already calls `resolve_with_sources()` (which assembles all layers) and reports every config key with its source — it is one logical step from also checking those keys against the tombstone registry and reporting hits. Adding a separate subcommand (`validate`) or a new top-level command (`doctor`) proliferates the config-inspection command surface.
findings.location: What section — 'Add a `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` command'; AC line 'A `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` command reports…'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `show_config.config_cli` to accept a `validate` positional (i.e. `rebar config validate`) that runs the tombstone check over env + TOML + `.rebar/config.conf` presence and exits non-zero on hits. This reuses the existing command wiring, the existing `resolve_with_sources()` call, and the existing `config_cli` argparse entry point — zero new top-level dispatch needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_gen_cli_reference.py` (to extend `_LADDER`), `src/rebar/_cli/help/` (for a new help text file), and `docs/cli-reference.md` to the Scope, and resolve the `rebar config validate` vs `rebar doctor` ambiguity so the exact CLI surface and its wiring path are specified.
findings.finding: The plan proposes both `rebar config validate` (a sub-subcommand of the existing `config` intercept arm) and `rebar doctor` (an entirely new top-level command) as implementation choices. A new top-level `doctor` command requires: (a) a new `src/rebar/_cli/help/doctor.txt` help file, (b) a new intercept arm in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py`, (c) adding `'doctor'` to `_LADDER` in `tests/unit/test_gen_cli_reference.py`, and (d) regenerating `docs/cli-reference.md`. None of these consumers are listed in the plan's Scope.
findings.location: What / 'Add a rebar config validate / rebar doctor command'; Scope section; src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py; tests/unit/test_gen_cli_reference.py:32-52

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why `RemovedInput` must be a separate dataclass rather than extending `Deprecation` with `removed=True` + `behavior='error'|'warn'` fields, OR explicitly state that a parallel registry is intentional and explain how the existing `test_deprecation_registry.py` completeness gate will be extended to cover tombstones.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `RemovedInput` dataclass alongside the existing `Deprecation` dataclass in `_deprecations.py`, but does not justify why a parallel type is necessary rather than extending `Deprecation` with `removed=True` and `behavior='error'|'warn'` fields. The existing dataclass already captures `kind`, `name`, `replacement`, `remove_in`, and `permanent`; the only structural addition is `behavior`. Introducing a second parallel registry violates the module's 'single source of truth' self-description without argument for why the distinction warrants a separate class.
findings.location: What section — 'Add a `RemovedInput` tombstone registry'; Scope — `src/rebar/_deprecations.py — add the tombstone registry alongside the alias registry`
findings.suggested_fix: Add `removed: bool = False` and `behavior: str = 'warn'` fields to the existing `Deprecation` dataclass (with `_removed_input()` factory helpers mirroring `_scheduled()`/`_permanent()`), so all removed surfaces live in the existing `_ENTRIES` tuple and the existing completeness test covers them automatically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the canonical `behavior` vocabulary for `RemovedInput` as a formal type (e.g. `Literal['error', 'warn']` or a small `Enum`) in the `_deprecations.py` Scope item, so the dispatch site and tests are written against a stable contract.
findings.finding: The `RemovedInput` schema's `behavior` field is referenced as an enum (`error|warn`) but its canonical vocabulary is never formally defined — it appears only as inline prose in bullet lists. No enum class, frozenset, or Literal type is specified anywhere in the plan for this two-value discriminator.
findings.location: What section — 'Add a `RemovedInput` tombstone registry (kind = env|cfg|file, name, replacement, removed_in, behavior = error|warn)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope item for `_deprecations.py`: '`RemovedInput.behavior: Literal["error", "warn"]` — "error" raises `RemovedInputError` (nonzero exit); "warn" emits a stderr warning and continues.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `behavior` dispatch protocol (error → raise `RemovedInputError`; warn → stderr + continue) lives as a method or function co-located with `RemovedInput` in `_deprecations.py`, so every check site (config.py, LLM path, validate command) calls a single canonical dispatcher.
findings.finding: The dispatch protocol for `behavior` (what the runtime does when `behavior == 'error'` vs `behavior == 'warn'`) is described in the 'What' prose section but is not specified to be co-located with the `RemovedInput` schema in `_deprecations.py`. The Scope item for `_deprecations.py` only says 'add the tombstone registry'; the Scope item for `config.py` says 'apply checks at the top of `load_config()`' — the decision rule bridging the schema to the action is split across two scope items with no explicit co-location requirement.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/_deprecations.py` and `src/rebar/config.py` scope items; What section 'behavior = error|warn'

findings.finding: AC3 ('Operationally-inert retired inputs warn rather than error') has no associated proving command or test specification. The table-driven test in AC2 covers error-classified entries; warn-classified entries (`REBAR_PUSH`, `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_MAX_RETRIES`, etc.) have no parallel test assertion specified, leaving the warn path unverified.
findings.location: AC3 ('Operationally-inert retired inputs warn rather than error')
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC2 table-driven test to include a second sub-table for warn-classified entries, asserting: exit code 0, stderr contains old name + replacement, process continues to normal completion.

findings.finding: The plan names the new CLI surface as `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` interchangeably with a `/` separator throughout the What, Scope, and AC sections, but never resolves which command is THE implementation — two different commands with different routing requirements in `_cli/__init__.py`.
findings.location: What section (bullet 4), Scope ('New CLI subcommand (config validate / doctor) + wiring'), AC line 5

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
